Yeah, be able to grow, scale. Do you know why he said that? Yeah, more clients. Guards up. Why? Oh, okay, do it again. No, no, why do you feel like he just gave you a surface-level answer? My tonality and maybe my pacing. Yeah, you just asked the question really fast. Yeah, you notice that? Now, this is constructive feedback, okay? Nobody's perfect here, but see how fast you asked the question. Yeah. And when you ask the question that fast, he doesn't have any time to really internalize what you're saying or asking. Most of the time, unless he's laid down, sale and desperate, he's going to give you a surface level answer like that. Yeah. Now, one thing I want you to be careful of, when you change the wording that I had there, like, like we do with our other clients, and you say, like we do with all of our other clients, that seems a bit, what?
It seems a bit like you're stretching that. Does that make sense? Yeah. But you don't want to say, like we do with some of our clients, because then that implies what? The sum don't get any results. So, the way I neutralize that, like we do with their other clients. Yeah. See the difference? Because if I said, like we do with all of our other clients, a lot of times they're going to be like, it sounds a little bit too much. So, I just take out that word all, like we do with their other clients. Yeah. Okay. So, okay. Just your average. Probably like nothing. Are you losing money? Losing money on the business. How much are you really losing? We've been spending $2K a month right now on ads. Okay, and what else are you losing per month besides the $2K a month in ads? We just rented an office space for $400 a month. Okay, and what else? These are just startup costs as well, Jeremy, like, you know, I'm expecting to lose a bit of money. I understand. Well, I guess if you want. What else are you losing? So, you're down about $2,400 a month. Yeah, if you put it that way. Do you have your phone on you? What's the math on that? What is, what's $2,400 a month times the, what is it, 12 times a year?
Let me check. $2,400 a month times a year. I mean, that's like $28,800. So, you're, you're, you're on pace to lose $28,800 if things keep going the same way. Okay. Why look at doing this now? Why not? Because a lot of people in your position, they might just like, you know, push it down the road and just kind of fold tent and go back to their job. I mean, for you, why do this now? Like, why not push it down the road and if it doesn't work out, just stay in your job? I can't afford to lose $128,000 over the next 12 months. Why? I don't even make that at my current job. Okay. Do you want to change that? Yeah, I do.
Why now? Because I'm sick of working for someone else. Okay. Time to make a change, possibly. It is. What if you don't do anything? What if you, what if you, you go to your spouse and, you know, because we're going to talk about funds you'll need to put into this if you really want to scale your business like or other clients. So, what if you go to your spouse and she doesn't want you to put in the funds to get there? Like, what would you do at that point? Well, I guess I'll just have to figure something else out. Well, what would you figure out? I guess just try to keep doing it myself, I guess. How's that working for you? I mean, does your spouse want you to get to 20 grand a month in profit?
Oh, yeah. Why? Well, she's the one telling me to actually, you know, quit my job so I can spend more time with the family, with the kids. Ah, okay. So, this would help her then by putting the funds in to get up to that level? Yeah, and then she'd be able to help me out as well and spend more time with me. What does it do for her though? You being able to get out of your job, like, how does that help her? Well, I'll be home more because right now I come home, I start work at 4 a. m., I come home at like 6 p. m., the kids are going to bed, I'm just, you know, she's been telling me for a while.
How long have you had to do that? Oh, man, like 12, 13 years. What's it doing to you? I'm exhausted. What's it doing to her? Yeah, she's more exhausted, yeah. Is she? She's over it. Okay, what had just happened? That all started by simply saying, is that your humble goal or would you rather do more? What would have happened if I just said, oh, four or five grand a month extra? Okay, ask the next question on my script. Maybe we get the sale, maybe we don't. Will we get this sale? I don't know, would we? Maybe. Would we? Would we or not? Yeah. Now, what did I do with the questions about his spouse? What was I doing there? Pre-handle. What was I doing? Pre-handle the objections.
I'm pre-framing there to handle what might come. What might come? His partner. Yeah, spouse. I need to talk to my spouse about it. Now, is he going to have to go? Is he going to say, oh, I don't know, Jim, I'm really going to have to go talk to her about investing this money now? Maybe, but very small chance. But after I built that big of a gap, even if he does, which is a very small chance, is it easier for me to help him overcome that? Yeah. Anthony, what did you notice? I think the biggest thing is, and a lot of people listening to that, they might say, oh, but Anthony was being easy. But I wasn't. I was giving him pushback at every phase.
So what Jeremy didn't do is he didn't let me off the hook. Did you guys hear that? He did not let me off the hook. And this is the biggest thing. He's asking questions to get a certain response. If he lets me off the hook, he doesn't get what the response he wanted. If I let him off the hook, I can't help him. That's the way I look at this. I don't necessarily look at this as like I have to get the right answers for me. I look at it as I have to get the right answers for him. Because if I let him off the hook, what's going to happen to this guy? In this example, he's gonna go out of business. And is his wife gonna be very happy with him?
Probably not. So am I helping him? By helping him relive the pain, am I helping him? By helping him see what the future pain will look like if he doesn't do anything, am I helping him or hurting him? I'm helping him. Why Why am I helping him by helping him see what the future will look like if he doesn't do anything? Because some of you might be like, I don't know, Jeremy. I feel uncomfortable asking my questions about their pain. Raise your hand if you've ever felt that way. I feel uncomfortable asking questions that are gonna help them feel pain. Raise your hand, just be honest. Okay. Because what happens if we can't help them relive pain? If we can't help them feel what future pain will feel like or see what future pain will feel like if they don't do anything?
What happens ultimately to that prospect? They have way more pain. They don't get their problem solved. They stay in the status quo and nothing ever changes. Whose responsibility is that? So who's really hurting them? The reason why sometimes we feel uncomfortable asking questions to help them relive the pain is that we feel uncomfortable with the sales techniques we've been taught to use. Would I be right? Because when I hear somebody say, like, oh, you know, I went to this thing and they did a pitch at it or whatever, I'm like, that's interesting they would view it that way because if those people didn't purchase that thing there, their problems wouldn't have been solved. They would have stayed in the status quo and nothing would have ever changed But it's how they view selling.
It's like pitching and selling and closing. We view selling, our clients view selling as helping the prospect go from where they're at now to where they want to be. Now, sometimes they don't know where they want to be because they don't know where they're at. Whose job is it to help them understand that? Us. Now can we explain that to them? Just tell them where they're at and tell them where they should be? Well, you can, you're just gonna play the what? Numbers game. But if you want to play the skills game like we did with Anthony and like you are as well, 16, well done. Could you have done that when you were 16? I don't think I could have. Well done.
What we just did there with Anthony is we've allowed him to persuade himself that he has to change. We've allowed him to see where he's currently at compared to where he really wants to be now. And that allows him to move forward and easily decide that he wants to change a situation. Remember, what's the two biggest emotional drivers that cause a human being to want to change? Pain and the fear of future pain, okay? If they don't, if we can't help them feel pain, they don't feel any need to change. Remember, there's that word change again And if they don't feel any need to change, they give us what? Objections. They don't buy. And we can control that. Okay? All right. Yeah? Round of applause. Round of applause, well done. Good job. That was really good actually, really good.
